		- INSIDE JOB
		- Businesses have limited liability
		- - Externalities - 3rd person who gets affected by a transaction they have not consented to e.g. getting someone else to build roads to drive your trucks on
		- In devastation there is opportunity - gold prices when twin towers hit
		- Advertising not a product but a way of life
		- Perception management - getting them to see you in the best light e.g. we live the same way you do, same activities, same lives, etc
		- Use of all senses e.g. 3rd McDonalds pos stands
		- Thelastfamily.com ideal self-sustaining home, costs, fencing security cams, live feed to police and internet, water collector and sun/wind power, electric car
		- Reason: privatisation of earths water, etc the corporation movie
		- Evil at the highest level - hostile takeovers using loans, agricultural bribes
		- Zeitgeist - give power to governments over corporations
		- Michael Moore - purpose, causing change
		- Vision: doing no harm
		- Social > personal dev app > e.g. using incentives (people liking/watching/subscribing to keep you staying to your goals or habit changing like bodyspace), punishments are more powerful though e.g. social backlash
		- Knowledge is power and with it comes great responsibility. you can be like a world religion, rejoice in the ignorance of not knowing anything and blind faith or be like a pioneer scientist and push the boundaries of human knowledge and understanding. You can chose to be the corporation who turns a blind eye to the environmental/social/health damage they are causing or be like the regulatory body who confronts these powerful organisations and will not tolerate their injustice anymore. With knowledge it is our responsibility to illuminate others to what they don't know in order to help them and others. We must strive to always learn more because even our current knowledge about a subject at any time is often incomplete and is unlikely to be fully understood within our lifetimes. Regardless we must base our actions on what we know and can foresee, always being open to alternate views and explanations as well as thoroughly testing our beliefs.
		- Passion is preferable word to purpose or meaning of life as who is to say we do have a purpose? I like to think of them of the same more or less but passion is more concrete, it is what we know excites us, gives us a reason to get up int eh morning and can thrive on without food/companionship and any other otherwise essential components to our life
		- About me - write out biography of how I got to here
		- My passion - at first I always thought that if I affected on life positively I would die happy
		- But I had people tell I've changed their lives, from showing them an alternate career path that has them doing their passion rather than we're used to already or what a guidance counsellor tells you you are suited to do, to simply providing comfort and a listening ear.
		- Then I decided I wanted to work on personal development, helping others achieve their own goals in improving themselves
		- I then asked why does it have to be such a personal journey? True we all have to walk our own paths and develop in different ways but we often travel parallel roads to each other and it is nice to have others travel with you both for morale support and simply companionship on this adventure called life
		- Now I am working towards world development, improving the world in which we live, to make it a better place for us. The world is a gift which we inherited from our ancestors, and it's only in the last few generations have we decided to go and mess it all up (and royally we have!). It is time to make a stand and stop this needless damage to our world and everything living in it. I've always loved the saying, "leave them better than when you found them", which applies to posessions borrowed, people met and the planet itself. Do the right thing and do good for your Earth, leave your legacy behind for the next generation. Thank you.
		- Life hacking, travel, social dev, world dev, influence
		- Positivity - appreciation article to let more come into life
		- Influence - reason to study it to protect ourselves form it despite knowledge still it makes it difficult to prevent the effects, to educate us, use? Everyone does it e.g. game, marketing, perception management, as long as it is moral, everything is grey area these days
	- Businesss notes
	  collapsed:: true
		- What do you want to do every day for the rest of your life?
		- Look out for opportunities e.g. people's complaints
		- You Must Always
		- Find A Market First...
		- And Then Concentrate On A Product!
		- Products are a dime a dozen. They are important but much less crucial to success than
		- finding a hot market. I'll tell you this: A guy with a new product cannot always find a hot
		- market for that product but a guy who has uncovered a HOT MARKET can always find a
		- product to fill the needs of that market.
		- - sell the thing first even if it doesnt exist yet!
		- - worst thing is building a product which has no demand
		- If you have multiple interests, either integrate it into a new niche OR start at one then just sidestep to another once you're an expert
		- - just try out different stuff that you like and continue moving towards that which you love and disregarding that which you don't
		- Do more stuff in the area that you want abundance in e.g. free speaking to get speaking opportunities
		- = Don't model the best as you can't get anywhere near them, model their mindset or for inspiration and model the HOW (rituals/habits) of someone just ahead of you
		- - the Giant's strategy was suitable for their time/opportunity
		- ECONOMICS - study of allocation of scarce resources
		- Scarcity determines cost (+ effort to produce this value) and in turn cost can signal scarcity
		- In long run competition reduces prices to marginal cost
		- High price results in lowest cost per customer  (maximised customer profit:w
		- - remember maximised profit can result in poorer user experience though
		- As a monopoly you want to reduce supply/output (scarcity) to keep maximise profit
		- - so can price discriminate causing customers to pay as much as they can/would
		- - use a Barrier to Entry e.g. first and only Life Development Program with patent/copyright
	- CCarter Big Brand - Checklist - How I Represent
	  collapsed:: true
		- http://www.wickedfire.com/enlightened-members/168970-ccarter-big-brand-checklist-how-i-represent-2.html
		- For my 1500 post, here is my new site checklist. I'm doing this, since I'm refreshing myself to destroy a lot of competitors in 2013. So, as I'm writing this, I'm following it on my newest site in one of the toughest industries ever. So I need to take advantage of every possible angle I can think of.
		- Every site I create, I want to have that big brand look and feel. Even my MFA(Made For Adsense) sites look like big brands. I started using responsive designs for all my new sites, since I also want to get that mobile traffic without an additional secondary site. In this tutorial, this is really for people that are new, and want to know what type of foundation I use to create my websites.
		- For the most part ALL my websites are Static sites using Perl/CGI for SSI (Server Side Includes), and the majority of the content is powered by wordpress internally. But for MFA sites I start with wordpress, and use only form fill out pages, like landing pages using SSI, you new kids can use your php if you would like.
		- Setup
		- I start off my websites using the Responsive template ([WordPress › Responsive « Free WordPress Themes](http://www.wickedfire.com/redirect.php?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwordpress.org%2Fextend%2Fthemes%2Fresponsive)). I always download the latest version of wordpress. The only plugins I use are Google Sitemap XML ([WordPress › Google XML Sitemaps « WordPress Plugins](http://www.wickedfire.com/redirect.php?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwordpress.org%2Fextend%2Fplugins%2Fgoogle-sitemap-generator%2F)) and Search Meter ([WordPress › Search Meter « WordPress Plugins](http://www.wickedfire.com/redirect.php?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwordpress.org%2Fextend%2Fplugins%2Fsearch-meter%2F)). I use Search Meter to understand what my visitors are searching for in my secondary search (onsite search), and then create content around that. Don't ask me why I only use those two plugins, I have my reasons. I don't waste time with all those SEO plugins, I just don't.
		- I alway setup my sites to configure with a CDN, usually CloudFlare for my MFA sites. Once I get my design where I want it, I make sure the Google Pagespeed score is at 90+. I am an extremist, so for my latest project I got that responsive design to 97/100. I also make sure I'm light as possible ([Web Page Analyzer - free website optimization tool website speed test check website performance report from web site optimization](http://www.wickedfire.com/redirect.php?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.websiteoptimization.com%2Fservices%2Fanalyze%2F)). Usually I make sure I have less than 20 https calls for each page. I'm a maniac like that. What you guys don't realize is that most of your websites are in the 100+ http calls for each page, crazy, whatever. The maximum I would allow for a page is 200K of total size. Anything above, that, there is something wrong. Usually my homepage is going to be the largest page.
		- I W3C Validate every page possible ([The W3C Markup Validation Service](http://www.wickedfire.com/redirect.php?url=http%3A%2F%2Fvalidator.w3.org%2F)). Get Firefox's Html Validator 0.9.5.2 Plugin. Everytime you view the source code, the errors and warnings are presented with fucking solutions people. I'm an extremist, so I put that validation on the highest Algorithm level = Serial - cause I'm serious people.
		- If you ever run into a website, that's pagespeed 95+, w3c validated, and is less thank 100K in size, and looks like a big brand, tread carefully, cause that sounds like one of my sites.
		- Logo
		- Get a logo from Fiver, not difficult.
		- Contact Form with Phone number and address
		- According to Google's lastest June 2012 leaked Manual Reviewer Guidelines ([http://www.wickedfire.com/traffic-co...es-2012-a.html](http://www.wickedfire.com/traffic-content/163861-leaked-google-quality-guidelines-2012-a.html)), When and if your site comes up for a manual review, 50% of the 161 pages talk about making sure the site looks legitimate, meaning does it have contact information. They look for phone numbers, addresses, privacy policy, and general stuff to make sure your site is a legitimate business.
		- CCarter spin, alright, so you're making a MFA site, make sure one your nameserver settings don't look like a joke. I once had a gaming website which I had to use as an emergency nameserver and forgot to change it back. Well that meant all new websites on that server has a nameserver setting of ns1.gamename.net, and ns2. Most people will look up your namesever settings as the start, so having a ridiculous nameserver settings doesn't help when you need to look legit. I use cloudflare.com for most of my MFA sites, so when anyone looks it up, is a bit more difficult for them to get a pinpoint of my server and at the same time, it gives it the look that I am serious about this site, since it's on a CDN. I use the free account version, end of story. Also, I recommend using whois privacy protection.
		- Get yourself a mailing box from a virtual office solution or the UPS store. Those addresses don't have P.O. Box in them, so you can use "suite 4500" or something. Looks good. If you can get one in every major city, and have the mail forwarded for a small fee of $30 to $100 a month, that's not bad to look like a company with multiple locations.
		- Google Voice or some 1-800 number voicemail service - pretty good. The freer the better. I recommend putting your phone number in the top right corner header area of every page. Looks legit. Right above it, a small link to the contact us page, with all the juicy contact information of your big brand's locations, and a nice contact form. Also, make sure to have a proper thank you page, and a thank you email. Protip, in the thank you email, you can upsell different CPA offers and test them out. I also recommend having a thank you page that has different upselling offers for CPA. Extra change in the bank. Have some sort of script that is monitoring everything and rotating offers in and out that are converting better than others. Always test.
		- I like using a services to setup as much Local SEO solutions for that site. Yes, it might be a MFA site or whatever, but I also want to look like a big brand. Who know it could turn into a real nice business or I might want to sell it. So, if I got a site that's setup using one of these local SEO services for $99 or $199 all going to my mailing addresses, then I've got that big brand look all over me. Imaging going to a site that has 5 different mailing address, and a 800 number. You would not question it twice, EVER. Since you got local SEO setup, have on your contact us page, a link to each location's Google Map. So now, you look balling.
		- Sign up Form
		- Whatever you are doing you should have as either a primary or secondary call to action for visitors to signup for something. Whether it's your community, email list, or just to stay in touch. If you have it as a secondary and not in your face like all those dumbass sites with the ajax box popup upon entering, then you'll actually start getting some signups. Now this is pretty controversial, but think about it when you go to a brand new website. Are you really likely going to simple fill out your information without knowing what the website is about? It's different when you go to Shoemoney or someone that you've read and heard about, maybe then. But generally, people coming from search engines are looking to find something first. So don't do that pop up shit, ever. That's interruption marketing ([My 1000th Post - Permission Based Marketing](http://www.wickedfire.com/enlightened-members/165991-my-1000th-post-permission-based-marketing.html)), and it's sucks.
		- Make sure do have the same thank you email and thank you page for this sign up form as well. Also, this is the best opportunity to up-sell different CPA offers in the email, and on the thank you page. You can have a small paragraph at the top, and then 3 different offers one on top of each other, with nice reviews from your staff or past customers (really you), next to them. looks legit.
		- Privacy, Disclosures, Disclaimers, and TOS
		- Privacy Policy: Google takes this into in their manual reviews. Have one. I have one that I just copy and paste the website's URL into, and set it and forget it. I no-index the page, just in case. I also no-index my contact page. I'm not a big fan of spam, so I don't index pages with forms on it, duh. If a page has to have a form on it, it's usually a 2 part form, with drop down menus as the beginning part. If I really need a page indexed and it has a form on it, I hide the form in an ajax call, so when javascript is disabled, no form. Obviously hackers that know how to use CasperJS, like yours truly, can get past this, but for the most part, I never ever get spam. Looks legit.
		- Disclosure: Since the FTC won't let me be, I put a clear Disclosure page, no-index as well if it's a MFA or CPA site. Something along these lines:
		- "In regards to the new FTC regulations, We are making this page to be compliant with 100% transparency regarding disclosure of incentive and paid reviews. Every page on this site has been created to generate revenue. This site generates income through banner advertisements and affiliate links on product reviews." - Stolen from JennyReviews.com.
		- Disclaimer I like adding this, just in case again. I also no-index this page as well, Something similar to this:
		- "Reviews on this site are merely opinions and may be moderated for any reason. The owner of this site is not help liable for any possible damages resulting from false claims as they may not be verifiable. We understand that not all merchants are the same. Having a negative experience with a single customer service representative may not reflect the quality of a product or entire company. Just remember to ultimately use your own judgment." - Stolen from JennyReviews.com.
		- Terms of Services: You already know what it is - Insert content here.
		- No-Index: Just a little background of why I No-Index pages, since they are not cached, and I ever have a situation, I don't necessarily want these particular pages to be readily available in a caching system. Most of the time I block them in the robots files, they are strictly for human eyes only.
		- Review / Testimonial page
		- I usually get about 10 fiver testimonials and either place them through the website. If I'm creating an MFA site, I have videos rotating in the sidebar or something. I also like putting my fiver videos on youtube from my own youtube channel - A couple should have your brand in the title, the rest can be longtail keywords you want to rank form. looks legit.
		- I also gather about 10 "testimonials" from people. cough… cough… that I use throughout my website. Looks legit.
		- Social Media
		- I Create a Facebook page for my site, 10 minutes. I create a twitter account with the brand's name (twitter.com/brandname). I create a Google Plus account (need this for future stuff - microdata/schema.org). I create a youtube, like mentioned before and put the Fiverr videos on there. Now, I want to make sure my brand is associated with these accounts, so I usually stick these silly social media icons in the Footer. Can you name one big brand, that doesn't have some social media presences? exactly. You want the big brand look. Social media icons is for big brands.
		- I don't waste time using the social site's javascript widget, since they are pretty slow at times, and slow down my site. If someone clicks on the icon, they'll go to the facebook, twitter, etc. themselves. Now, what they find on that is sometimes more telling. Since you want to look like a big brand, you've got to get followers. How do you get followers? Buy them. I use Fiverr again for this. I really don't give a fuck if they are not really, what I need to do is first look legitimate to get pass any curious onlookers, just in case. At this point, it's beyond getting past Google manual review, it's about getting past people like Guerilla and his Community SEO guidelines. See, if I can get past a reviewer of a manual edited directory, then I'm definitely big brand material. So, I buy fiver gigs that will get my to the 20K fans on facebook, and 50K followers on Twitter. I make sure to target USA facebook profiles on 80 to 90% of them. I end up getting about 50 to 60%, so that's perfect. Same thing with twitter, I blast it.
		- This is purely a psychological game at this point, yes, Facebook and Twitter will delete accounts, and churn shit every now and then. But at most i've seen is a drop in 25% to 30%. So, at 20K facebook fans, After 3 or 4 months, I'll end up with about 14K to 15K left. Now what's interesting is that real people will become fans of pages with 5 to 10K and over. So you'll start growing a real fan base. I rarely buy anything that touches Google directly, like Google Plus likes, or fans, whatever Matt Cluts and those clowns are doing now-a-days.
		- I then setup Hootsuite with my Google Plus, Facebook, Twitter, and Linkedin account for a scheduled post for the next 90 days. (The free account lets you do that). Now, this is my tactic, Since my social media accounts are setup backlinking to my website, I don't want to promote my website for the next 90 days, that's boring. So I find news topics of competitors, and topics in my niche. Why? Cause that shows I'm a hub for the niche, and at the same time shows I am outlinking to relevant topics. This helps establish my authority in the niche. So, I do 4 twitter a day, 1 facebook post a day, 3 linked posts a day, and 1 Google Plus post a day to topics in my niche. News is always the best, since it's fresh off the press, and you can get some traffic boosts from that when people click on it. If something in your niche is trending on Twitter or Google plus, cross promote it through hootsuite to your other accounts.
		- Only after 90 days do I start inserting links to my content, and even then it's only every 1 out of 10 posts. Also, having fresh content on your Google Plus account is necessary, and seems to be a factor, in getting your Google Plus photo to show up in the SERPS, that's why I go hard at the very beginning, and forever. So now, my social media accounts are authoritative, and I am relevant since I'm talking about big brands. Over 10K fans on my main social media, and daily updating content on them, looks legit.
		- I recommend using getting a Knowem profile setup for all the major social sites, I think there is like 150 or 300 packages.
		- Microdata
		- You ever notice those SERP images of authors? Yeah, that through Microdata and Google Plus. Using the Rich Media Snippet Tool to verify, you can use the Schema.org markups on your website and use your Google Plus for authorship.
		- Google Plus Authorship:
		  collapsed:: true
			- Get a Google Plus Account.
			- Login to Google Plus.
			- Click "Edit Profile" (Above your big banner).
			- Click on "About" (Underneath your big banner).
			- Goto the "Contributor to" section and Click on it.
			- Click "Add custom link", and add your home page link, and click Save.
			- Click "Done Editing".
			- Goto your website, and create a backlink to your Google Plus Account in the footer area of your website. (The reason I say the footer area, is because for every page it's on, your profile will be considered the authorship). Make sure to use the rel="author" element in your ahref, so it should look like this:
		- <a rel="author" href="https://plus.google.com/XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX">Gay Webmaster</a>
		  collapsed:: true
			- To Verify everything is correct visit [Google Structured Data Testing Tool](http://www.wickedfire.com/redirect.php?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.google.com%2Fwebmasters%2Ftools%2Frichsnippets), and input your url, and click preview.
		- You should need your Google Plus Profile appearing as the authorship. You can use this tool to also verify the way Google sees the Microdata/schema markups on your site.
		- Microdata/Schema: Taking shit to the next level. I mark up my addresses, phone numbers, and everything possible. This way, as new technologies get introduced that utilizes these markups, I'm ahead of the curve. I've been using Schema markups for two years now. All of a sudden, Google's ancillary services are adapting more and more to these markups, and I'm getting the aged benefits. Visit Schema.org for more on this.
		- Search
		- This is where a majority of people fail. Have a fucking search engine on your site. That secondary search is key, since you can log the search terms that people us, and create new content accordingly. People are type in what they are looking for on your site, why the fuck aren't you taking advantage of this? One a month look at what they typed in, and create content accordingly. jeez. (This is where the Search Meter plugin for WordPress comes into play).
		- FAQ
		- I love FAQs, cause you can put in upselling potentials in here. An FAQ page with 10 to 12 questions is perfect. Make sure the question is actually answering a person's question, and not just self promotion. Also, make sure they are questions people would really ask instead of what you think people care about your brand. They don't give a shit about your brand, they want their question answered. This is a great page for testimonials as well. Upsell in the sidebar or have a signup for in the sidebar as well.
		- Content
		- I always have my homepage look like a big brand, meaning big image/photo at the top, maybe a slider, with a call to action.
		- Home Page, Since we are discussing a MFA, make that shit into a 1800 to 2500 word post. That shit is going to rank. I like putting 5 additional pages with 1500+ words for my top level/categories pages. Throw in your video testimonials on each one, and some of your written customer testimonials. When writing content, use sub sections, and here is the true fucking purpose of the h2, h3, h4, h5, h6 tags. Sub sections are great for humans therefore also search engines.
		- People love bullet-points and lists. 90 to 100% of your articles should have bullet-points, and should use sub sections (can use numbered listed in substitute of bullet-points). Every piece of content should have 1 to 3 photos. Most people are lazy, and put one photo at the top. I spread 3 photos throughout the content. Sometimes I substitute a video for a photo. Know a trick to up-selling? For every photo, use a caption. EVERYONE reads the caption of a photo, they just do. That's the one thing they will read, so use them right underneath the photo, and upsell.
		- BlockQuotes, are perfect for focusing on features, and getting the attention of the visitors, use them. Put Quotes from authority people in your content and backlink to their website (Outbound links are important people).
		- So now your MFA site has a homepage with 1800 to 2500 words, 5 additional pages with above 1500 words. Now go to town with content. I create 100 posts of content from 300 to 800 words and drop 40 of them when I launch the website, then drip the next 60 for the next 60 days. Usually these are longtail keywords that have anywhere from 1 to 20 people a day (EXACT match) look for them. After 3 months I've got 2000 to 3000 people a day coming to the site just from that. (Content at $1/100 words should be around $800 to $900. You can probably get away with just the first 6 pages, homepage and 5 long pages for MFA sites, but that added 100 post is going to take over your niche and get all the bottom feed content. This is critical for your sitelinks, since it's based off of traffic!
		- Once, I got the first 6 pages up, and all the offsite setup, I setup Google WMT (Webmaster Tools) for the site, and occasionally setup up the sites on Binghoo as well.
		- Lists
		- Whethere it's a top 10 list, people love that shit. They'll sit there and read each list. Make sure you have photos corresponding for each list item.
		- Authority Logos
		- Logos like the BBB, Authorize.net, Verisign, TrustGuard, McAfee, and HackerSafe are huge when helping you look like a legitimate site. Put them on there, whether you actually are a customer of their or not… that's up to you. Use industry and niche logos. Just put them on your site, and never mention the name of the companies anywhere. Never use the name of the company as the saved filed name either. That way it's a little harder to get that call from citigroup, about taking the name off, eventually someone might come asking you to take their name down, just do it - It's usually fucking CitiGroup, those of you in the financial industry know exactly what I am referring to). Also, I recommend getting your own IP and having SSL installed. When people fill out forms on your site, make sure they are doing it on the SSL version. If that's not legit I don't know what it.
		- I usually wait till I'm getting 100 people a day before putting Adsense on my sites. If it's a CPA review site, then make sure to NEVER EVER use a naked link. Re-route that shit through javascript or something Protip: You can track better which links are getting click this way
		- Now From here, do whatever you want with your big brand, this is a lot of work, but now you've got a website that looks legit, and you can go to town with your BST spammy $5 link package buys (obviously you should wait till you pass the 1K visitors a day mark). Since you've got serious social media offsite with fans, and lots of rich content and videos, with contact information - phone number, address, and all the necessary privacy disclosures, you'll pass any manual review, whether it's Google and maybe Guerilla's
		- Let's review:
		  collapsed:: true
			- Fast Website - Pagespeed, CDN, W3C, Lightened, Proper Nameserver
			- Responsive Design - Mobile and Tablet Ready without losing functionality
			- Contact Info - Phone, Physical Addresses, & Map (Multiple Locations - With Local SEO setup for each)
			- Logo
			- Sign Up Form
			- Necessary Privacy, Disclosure, and Disclaimer
			- Reviews / Testimonials
			- Social Media - Setup on All major sites + Knowem for your brand with 90 Days worth of content to pages within your niche (Authority, trust, and relevancy building up), with 10K+ followers/fans.
			- Google Plus Authorship / Microdata Schema for future technologies.
			- Sitelink ready (won't happen the first days obviously) - pretty site structure with breadcrumbs.
			- FAQ section.
			- Tons of content (80,000+ words) - targeting long-tail, so you have a solid foundation of 2000 to 3000 visitors a day.
			- Authority Logos - Look legit.
			- SSL installed.
		- Everything you need to appear to be a big brand, with traffic, social signals, and all. Good luck bros.
		- Hopefully, this is a nice condensed version of stuff that will refresh people or help newbies. Also, I would love to have serious feedback, if you can poke holes in this, is only helps me comeback with a stronger solution that will make this more bulletproof.
		- P.S. Some of this stuff has been mentioned on the Internet and WickedFire in particular before, especially the stuff about the Internet and websites.
		- Good luck bros.
		- Originally Posted by malloc How are you picking your niche and how to you gauge competition?
		- It doesn't really matter what niche I go after. This works in payday loans, auto insurance, insurance, mortgage, or whatever. Recall, I'm using the longtail game plan of getting keywords with to 1 to 20 a visitors day. Multiply that by 100 posts, that comes out to the 2000 visitors a day once all the posts are up, just in longtail. This all can be achieved in 3 months tops. I have a serious game plan once I hit the 2000K visitors a day, and that's going after the city/state/zip code variations.
		- Let's say I'm going after mortgage, Adwords CPC of $50+. So my MFA site can get lets say, conservatively $2 per click (obviously $50 would be great, but lets use $2). If 1% of those 2000 visitors click on my ads, I'm estimating $40 a day in revenue, and that's being conservative at $2 per click. 1 month = $1200 in revenue, just from Adsense.
		- Since I'm smart, I'm most likely got a lead capture form, and are selling the leads to a mortgage company for $10 a lead. If 1% of those 2000 visitors fill out a form (my average is 13 to 15% for these type of sites, but let's use 1%), thats $200 a day in revenue from lead sales. 1 month = $6000 in revenue from Selling Leads.
		- Then there are my CPA offers. If I'm averaging $28 for a CPA offer that directly related to mortgages OR indirectly like a Credit Check, hint, hint, and With all the additional upsells like the thank you email and thank you pages, as well as sidebar upsells I get lets say 0.5% of the 2000 visitors thats $8,400 in 1 month from CPAs.
		- So with 2000 visitors a day, and combining the conversion rate of all offers equaling 2.5% I'm making $15,600 in revenue from 3 different sources off of the stuff I did. And recall, that's conservative on the payouts, conversion rates, and CPC. That's the real end goal. Big branding is all gravy, but the long-tail goal of getting to the 2000+ visitors a day is where they money is at. It really doesn't matter what niche I go after, anyone can dominate with long-tail without sweating. No one wants to say this, but this is how most of these kids are making their money. Easy shit like this.
		- I gauge the competition using Marketing Samurai, Google's LOL generator, WordTracker, and Term Explorer. Really, I only look at the EXACT matches. I really use Marketing Samurai to get terms, and WordTracker for the KEI3 stats. I'm not looking for short tail shit, cause some of my niches are regulated, and I don't need those regulator clowns on my ass. When shit gets good, $15K a month, flip that site for 12x the earns (~100K) Go on vacation for the next 9 months, and come back next year and do it again.
		- Quote:
		- Originally Posted by skohh +1 on term explorer, I've been using it since beta up to present day. It's really come far. If you're going after the long tail game, I think it's a must have. Just throwing that out there.CCarter, you don't mention much about building links. How can you compete long tail in payday loans without links. I have to crank out a lot of links to do long tail in that niche, as you may already know. Talking like the 2k Exact match long tails. Super competitive. Maybe you covered that somewhere else.Pen names always work?
		- When I'm doing longtail I'm going after really low hanging fruit, 20 people a day comes out to 600 people a month for Exact. 2K a month is too much. Even 10 people a day comes out to 300 people a month for Exact. At 300 people a month, with lets say a 20% increase for additional surrounding keywords (Broad/Phrase) that's 360 for that one longtail. I do a 100 articles of those, and i'm 36,000 people a month. That's 1200 people a day with no backlinks. You don't need backlinks if you go for the really low hanging fruit.
		- See, the problem is no one is going for these terms, so they come in with, well let me go for 2K/volume per month for my long-tail phrases - that's 67 people a day. Well, that's what everyone is doing, since they are not going to go after the short-tail version of those terms because it's too difficult (Example Payday Loan). No one wants to go after terms that have 1 to 20 people a day looking for them (Example: Payday loan In Omaha, Nebraska - I am making that shit up to show you how in-depth I would get, I don't know the volume). Even 1 person a day looking for a payday loan. If you can answer their question and only that one person converts, thats 30 people a month off of one keyword.
		- I see it all the time, everyone wants 50+ people a day, but it's payday loans. I don't consider anything over 50+ people a day really long-tail in my book. That's still volume. You need to get the 1 to 5, then 5 to 10, then 10 to 20 keywords, that everyone else thinks is garbage. Remember, it's an algorithm, once you accumulate 100 people a day from these longtails, then go after 10 to 20, and get to the 1000K mark. Then go for 20 to 40.
		- You ever see those websites with almost no backlinks then look at their analytics and see 10K daily visitors in tough niches? All that shit is long-tail, bottom of the bottom feeding longtail. When you are at this level, backlinks aren't necessary. Then as you grow, you can come in with backlinks etc, if necessary, but you probably won't need too.
		- I'm doing a case study, I'll share the results with you once I get it going to show you exactly the type of terms I'm going after. 2K longtail sounds like 3 or 4 word keywords. I research 3 to 6 keyword terms. You can get ideas from ubersuggest.org - shoutout to [aim100](http://www.wickedfire.com/members/aim100.html).
		- Yeah Pen name is cool.
		- Quote:
		- Originally Posted by AdamC Do you bother with a places page(s), google/yelp etc reviews?
		- Yeah, Using some Local SEO services, they usually handle this stuff. Google Places is where I mention the maps portion.

		- The old ape is just about to start a new article series and this time we are going down the rabbit hole that is the process of deep content creation.

	- GO TO MEETUPS IN COMPLIMENTARY INDUSTRIES.
	  collapsed:: true
		- Right after I got back from Thailand I went to a social media marketing event. I told people I ran a small SEO business, and in 2 hours I had 3 job offers. Most of these people were doing social media, design, PPC, etc. They didn’t specialize in SEO, but they had clients who needed it. The more people they can turn to outsource that work the better – especially if you can be reliable.
		- If you’re doing web design, go to a conference or meetup for coders or developers. Do these two things and you’ll be swimming in clients in no time.
		- Start small with your clients first. Regardless of which of those five skills you decide to specialize in, you’re still going to be learning. Don’t over promise anything you know you can’t do.
		- That said, you don’t have to know everything either. Be upfront with your clients about the work you’ll be providing, and in time you’ll begin to see referrals, a better reputation, and more money.

	- I should have shot commercials last year
	  collapsed:: true
		- A week or two ago, I finally commissioned a video to help promote Amazon For A Penny. I figured that it would be a fun way to boost traffic and liven up the site. At the worst it would provide me with a nice backlink from YouTube.
		- In fact, I did the whole video for less than ten dollars. Even if you’re super cheap you can browse Fiverr and find some decent talent. For five bucks it’s entirely possible to make a full length commercial for whatever you want.
		- Hire professionals.
		- Commercials are easy to write. Instead I just wrote down the dialogue and who spoke it. The whole thing was written within about five minutes.
		- If you have a website or sell a product, I would recommend making at least one video. It makes the site more dynamic and sets your content apart from everyone else.
	- internet marketing info
	  collapsed:: true
		- I think this is the same info as 'B.S. Your Way to Internet Riches' or similar
		- Technical information
		- If you want to work at Internet marketing, you have to bite the bullet about learning technical skills. There are many, too. Fortunately, there is a lot of automated stuff out there that can be used to make money while you are learning the other stuff.
		- One of the most important technical skills you need all the way from the beginner level, one I have never seen discussed, is fabricating and operating aliases. You need to learn to make up people and act as if you are them. The only person who does not need to do this is one who strives to become an authority and only work within that field. If you want to sell a large quantity of stuff, though, and you are not Wall Mart, you will need to use other names so you don’t blow your authority work. It would never do for an expert in diets and weight loss to sell candy.
		- I will not go into many specific details, but keep to broad outlines as I have done up to now.
		- If you have difficulty with imagination, there is a small resource called Fake Name Generator (look it up). It will give you name, address, phone number, credit card number, etc., all fake but in the correct format. (Obviously the credit card doesn’t work.) That’s a start. Get a real phone number for free online from K7 if you feel you need it (look it up). Otherwise, get a Google account (or similar), an email account, and whatnot. Then start signing up for a My Space account and other Web 2.0 sites. The more stuff you get, the more credible your new alias becomes.
		- You should also keep some kind of records, maybe a spreadsheet folder for each alias. Otherwise, keeping track will be hard after he/she grows up and you have other aliases taking off.
		- I do not suggest taking this concept so far as to procure a real SSN for your alias or things like that. You do not need the authorities after you. All you want to do is make money and provide value in return, not break any laws by falsifying government documents.
		- But playing around with this, for example, getting your photo from public domain photo banks, making an avatar, etc., can be a hoot. A money advantage that’s a hoot is using your community of aliases to plug each other’s products and start drawing attention to the products. This sales force can get hilarious.
		- Backlink strategies
		- Writing
		- One-page content blogs or Web 2.0 pages for backlinks to a niche minisite. Use an anchor text with keyword in your link and write something relevant to that keyword and your site. That way the search engines will not penalize it like they do with spam. Rework an article from the minisite and you are done.
		- Article for article directory. These articles are used for content on sites, blogs, ezines, etc., by people who can’t or won’t write what they need. The article includes an author’s box that needs to be published with the article as the price the user pays for using it. The author’s box includes a link to a site you own, preferably a money-site.
		- The whole purpose of writing an article for directories is to get the reader to keep moving down the page on into the author’s box and click on the link. There is a very good formula I came across for this.
		- All articles are niche articles by definition. The most important overall point is to give the reader enough information about his interest to keep him reading, but not enough to satisfy him (or bore him).
		- You can use this system for reworking scraped content, but I will give the system as if the article were being written from scratch.
		- Before research: Make a list of 3 keywords or keyword phrases you want to use throughout the article.
		- Research: Use one of the keywords on a Google search and open several sites from the first page. (Or use scraped content, PLR, etc.) Read them quickly, partially or superficially to get a general idea of a point. Write that point down. Get one point for your article from each source and make a list of 3-5 points. Do not copy the other content, but keep the page open for checking facts while you are writing the article.
		- Title, aim for:
		- 1. Curiosity (“secret,” “hidden,” etc.),
		- 2. Shock value,
		- 3. Benefit (“how to,” “discover,” “7 Ways” etc.) or
		- 4. Ask a question.
		- Any one of these is good. You don’t need to use more than one. Include your main keyword.
		- Paragraphs: In the outline below, each number should be one or two sentences, three maximum.
		- First paragraph:
		- 1. General idea of the subject.
		- 2. List an abbreviated form of your points.
		- 3. Statement of article’s purpose (“explore,” “compare,” “discuss,” etc.)
		- Point paragraphs (3 to 5):
		- 1. Give point (in different words).
		- 2. Two or three informative sentences about that point.
		- 3. Conclusion and transition to next point. Note: in the last paragraph, mention that the points are over.
		- Last paragraph:
		- 1. General statement about what was presented.
		- 2. Sum up the points covered (abbreviated), but mention some things you didn’t cover. Focus on things the reader really wants to know.
		- 3. Sum up article based on the purpose given in the first paragraph.
		- Author’s box:
		- 1. Give something, but minimum information on author. The less the better. This bores folks. Picture is OK.
		- 2. Use anchor texts in links to your site or sites.
		- 3. Give an idea of what to expect on your site or sites.
		- 4. Give a good reason (benefits) for going to your site. Hint that the missing info in the article is there. Say you have ironclad systems or secret solutions and other bullshit (but have something like that up when they get there). Offer freebies.
		- Notice how this drives the reader down the page in a logical manner and pushes him to click on the link.
		- The article should be about 500-1000 words. If you do shorter, the reader feels cheated or the information was too superficial for him to click on your link, and if you do longer, he might put the article down before getting to your link. Make your keyword density about 3%.
		- Review article. This more or less follows the principles given for directory marketing articles, but there are some differences. There are three kinds of reviews and all of them aim at getting the reader to click on a link to the sales page of a product. Always use anchor text in links. Period. No exceptions. A banner or two on the side of the review is OK (or above or below it). A picture of the product is good to include in the reveiew.
		- Review articles are used more on review minisites than anywhere else. These minisites are actually niche presales sites in drag. If you do present a review as a guest on someone else’s blog or publication, link to your niche presales site and not directly to the sales letter for fewer headaches with site/publication owners.
		- 1. Normal review. Give product and some basic specs. Mention problems it solves. Bullshit some personal story (or stories) about how you or someone you knew had a problem, the more personal the better, and the product helped solve it or made the person rich or whatever. Give a minor issue or two that the product doesn’t solve so you sound objective. Hint that there are other great benefits (minor ones) you have heard about but still need to look into. Then strongly endorse the product as a problem solver. Click here. You are done.
		- 2. Comparative review. This is actually 3 or more reviews of similar products with some kind of rating system. Do no more than five products at a time. Use the normal review system, but elaborate more on the product you want to sell. Make sure second place is really close and there is at least one with a rather poor rating in third or fourth or fifth place.
		- 3. Scam review. Two of the most searched-for words on search engines are “review” and “scam,” so use this traffic. (Bonus is also high up.) Sound like a hard-hitting journalist at the start. You want the bloody facts and nothing more. You’re nobody’s fool. Say you heard a lot of gossip about the product being a scam and you are going to get to the bottom of it. Then really bullshit a personal story about you or someone you know using the theme of “I was lost but now I’m found.” Life was hell before the product. Someone mentioned it to help you. You were suspicious as all get-out. You tried the product with misgiving. Voila. Problem solved. Life ain’t all roses now, but you are no longer a sufferer and you’ll be damned if you’ll ever talk bad about that product again. Yada yada yada… click here…
		- Normal content articles and books. Go to school to learn how to do this or use other people’s stuff. Blog posts and forum posts for content are about the same. You do this stuff because you love it, not to sell stuff. So this is outside of my discussion.
		- Style.
		- 3. If you get stuck, ask a question and answer it. (“Why do you want to do that? Well…”)
		- 4. Spin a “most people” bullshit story. (“Most people say this…” or “Most gurus say that…” followed by “But the truth is…”) There is no way on earth you could know what most people say, but it makes for great bullshit. Since you are giving The Hidden Truth, you are the Hero of the Day and the reader is pleased to know you.
		- The BS Riches Tributary
		- Don’t mention you name at first in the bright lights. Focus on the system. When you talk about yourself (or “others” talk about you), say you have been in reclusion for 5 years or more, during which time you have been working on and perfecting this top-secret system. Only now have you felt it could be unleashed on the public. Say you tried it out first and made a fortune with it, so now you want to spread the good times around and share it with others. Talk about the virtue of giving something back for your blessings. Make up stuff, texts, photos, videos, etc., about how much money you have and show it off.
		- Start putting your aliases to work. Make them do it in any manner you want (endorsement, pure sales page, reviews, “is it a scam?” etc.), but the final message has to be, “Oh Wow! This is IT!” Mix it up and have a ball. Other people will start jumping on the band-wagon after a while.
		- Once you start making some money, get in touch with another guru (or more than one) and offer him a cut or outright money for plugging it to his customers and plugging it in general. His name will bring others and the whole shebang is on its way in the BS Riches Tributary.
		- The present average price for an ebook is around $47 and that’s pretty good. If you go much cheaper, you lose image and market focus. A dude who forks over 47 smackeroonies for a piece of crap like a PDF ebook that he has to print out when he can go down to the bookstore and buy a new attractive bound book for 25 bucks is probably willing to dig deep for other crap that makes him look like an insider.
		- Also, you might want to increase the perceived value of your product by saying the real price is $97, but for a limited time, yada yada yada…
		- Once your fish bites the bait and hook, you start reeling him in. This will be far less than 1% of all people who look at your stuff, but this small group is where your BS Riches lies. Sell your customer the dream. Sell him the sizzle. Tell him he is going to get rich beyond decency and belong to the world’s elite of insiders. Sell him on His Special Fine Destiny.
		- Don’t think he’s a total fool, though. He might be committed because of the 47 smackeroonies, but he wants to be told he did a good job and he wants some insider info.
		- So pepper the ebook (or software manual) with phrases like “Congratulations!” “You did it!” “Get ready for a wealth make-over!” “This will blow your mind!” and so on. Be generous. After all, the dude paid for this. Make him think he already has money in the bank.
		- Show him your rich-dude stuff as proof (young pretty women, fancy cars, yacht, mansion, etc.) If you are starting out and don’t have jack, make this stuff up. There are photos all over the Internet and if you can’t copy-paste, hang it up. What the hell are you reading this for?
		- Tell him to change his mindset, to start thinking about how he wants to spend his new riches and pay off all his debts. Give him a generous dishing of bullshit mental images about his wife smiling, friends laughing, “debt-free” signs, telling his boss off, cars, houses, jewelry, hundred dollar bills floating around, the works. Hit him hard, but don’t go overboard. Even a duped fool has a bullshit meter and you never want that to go into the red zone.
		- Now you have to provide some value. Mostly tell him how to sell some stuff and how drive traffic to the sales points. You might want to tell him how to make some stuff to sell or how to get some stuff from other people. There are tons and tons of “secrets” out there, so use your imagination. If you get really stuck, focus on a small item, like an Adwords title for instance. Crib from the literature, but use your own words. Write 7 pages on how to do the title, whether to use a call to action or a question mark or any other bullshit you can think of. Use some big words if you know of any for statistics, but keep the insider jargon flowing in abundance.
		- There is another angle. A person who is reading a PDF file that he paid $47 bucks for is paying attention. You have a captive audience, so make a sales pitch. Tell him that he can raise his earnings “up a notch,” or “take it to the next level” or some other bullshit phrase that sounds good. Then offer him some other stuff. Make sure your pitch about his imaginary wealth takes it to the next level, too, so he thinks he is going to get even richer than before.
		- I read in one place that this should observe the proportion of 20% information value, 40% selling the dream and 40% sales pitch for new stuff. That doesn’t sound right from the stuff I have seen, but the categories are correct. To make an off-the-cuff estimation, I have seen it more like 50% information value, 30% selling the dream and 20% sales pitch for new stuff or something along those lines. This should be tested and varied according to results.
		- The next step (the new stuff you stick him with) is to get him signed up into a membership site where he can download videos, mp3’s, transcripts and other people’s leftover stuff for free. Charge a sign-up fee of $100-200 and a monthly fee of $20-40.
		- Once inside, he can also buy a special DVD course for $497 or so. Or he can get the entire multimedia home-study course (including a 600 page loose-leaf binder text) for $1,997.
		- Change these prices if they don’t work or if you think you can get more.
		- Give your disciple (he is now a disciple) more secrets and start peppering in Black Hat info. But keep the jargon and the bullshit flowing. Remember, this is a tributary and you are now in the river part. So flood the bullshit. He is now an insider and he needs to be treated as such.
		- Then shoot for the moon: a few one-on-one coaching sessions with membership to a special insider’s club with group coaching and workshops. Make sure your insider’s club can be accessed from inside the first membership site with aggressive “Members Only” type gateways. Here you can get $20,000 or so a pop.
		- What about traffic for all this crap?
		- Well, there’s the normal stuff you teach (SEO, backlinks, PPC, etc.), but there’s a whole lot more.
		- Make an aggressive affiliate recruiting campaign. Like I mentioned above, put your aliases to work as your first affiliates. Make them sound like they are selling the hell out of your product. Make it easy for new affiliates to sign on. Offer free training and teach new affiliates how to spam the living daylights out of the Internet, sites, blogs, forums, audio, video, the works. But always say on your own sales material that you are “100% against spam and unconditionally condemn the practice.”
		- Give the new affiliates pre-written texts so all they do is say good things about you all over the place. Give them banners and sales buttons and templates. Cloak their hoplinks for them. Offer them a 75% commission. Keep your aliases pumping right along beside them and if an affiliate starts selling well, copy all his sales stuff for one of your aliases and do the same.
		- You will meet many people along the way and other bullshit artists. Use them for ideas. It shouldn’t be hard to come up with an unending list of new products and campaigns. Before too long, you will have a name as an Internet guru, you will be invited as speaker to prestigious seminars and conventions, you will be a world-class authority and your trip on the BS Riches Tributary will have reached the ocean.
		- By this time, it doesn’t matter whether you are White Hat, Gray Hat or Black Hat. You are king.
		- THE END

	- Linchpin
	  collapsed:: true
		- Linchpin
		- Useful questions to ask yourself:
		- “What is my art?”
		- “What should I do next?”
		- “What am I resisting?”
		- “Why am I resisting?”
		- “What do I fear?”
		- “I can’t … or I don’t want to?”
		- “What would make me impossibly good at my job?”
		- “How can I be more artistic, motivated, aware, and genuine?”
		- “Am I moving?”
		- “Am I exerting emotional labor?”
		- Linchpins do two things:
		- exert emotional labor
		- make a map
		- Things that make you indispensable:
		- Providing a unique interface between members of the organization; organizations are networks
		- Delivering unique creativity
		- creativity: personal, original, unexpected, useful
		- unique creativity: requires domain knowledge, a position of trust, the generosity to actually contribute; implies that the creativity is focused and insightful.
		- delivering unique creativity: the hardest of all; you also ned to be passionate enough to risk the rejection that delivering a solution can ring; you must ship!
		- Managing a situation or organization of great complexity; “When a situation gets too complex it’s impossible to follow the manual, because there is no manual.” Mapmaking and clear judgement must be practiced.
		- Leading customers. “[E]very person who interacts with a customer (or a business being sold to, or a donor to a nonprofit, or a voter) is doing marketing as leadership.”
		- Inspiring staff: a team at rest tends to stay at rest; your job is to make something happen.
		- Providing deep domain knowledge: deep domain knowledge is rarely enough to become indispensable; combine it with smart decisions and generous contributions, though, changes things
		- Possessing a unique talent
		- When you meet someone, you need to have a superpower. If you don’t, you’re just another handshake. It’s not about touting yourself or coming on too strong. It’s about making the introduction meaningful. If I don’t know your superpower, then I don’t know how you can help me (or I can help you).
		- To be a linchpin, your power must be very difficult to replace.
		- Two choices:
		- Develop the other attributes that make you a linchpin
		- Get a lot better at your unique talent
		- The challenge, then, is to be the generous artist, but do it knowing that it just might not work. And that’s okay.
	- Money highway - traffic, conversion, retention and new income
	- Marketing methods
	  collapsed:: true
		- [http://www.smartpassiveincome.com/how-to-get-on-the-front-page-of-slideshar/](http://www.smartpassiveincome.com/how-to-get-on-the-front-page-of-slideshar/)
		- “Not measuring. How many books did promotion X sell?  I know, as an example, which offers in my [“land rush” competition](http://www.fourhourworkweek.com/blog/2010/12/09/the-land-rush-4-hour-body-book-promotion/) converted browsers to buyers most effectively, as I used SlideShare.  How many people watched the slideshow and then bought?  It was the 3-book and 30-book packages.  I could then focus on these for a follow-up promotion, which sold more than 4,000 books on Amazon in less than 20 hours.  Measure the ROI of your campaigns or fail — that’s the choice.”
		- speeches - tedx, universities e.g. reading alumni, UCL; businesses e.g. IBM
		- iTUNES PODCAST - Intro musicFiverr - voiceover guy for introduction
		- COLLABS
		- *Interviewing experts about relevant topics for a podcast? Success stories or background info or flesh out a topic
		- Further resources for those interested? -My influences and relevant experts
		- NEXT BIG THING
		- Utilise creative and determined user base and claim commission like kickstarter
		- Leaderboard competitions
		- Contests for prizes by them providing value eg how many people can you mentor or help fulfill their goals
		- FB PAGE Add your image ensuring you always use your keywords as the name of the image, username as kw and
		- http://www.smartpassiveincome.com/facebook-landing-page-html/
		- Press Releases
		- Press releases are a slight variation on the article submission process, but should have the same effects. For everything new that you add to your site, write a quick press release for it. Writing a press release is a different style which you’ll need to adapt to. Here’s a good guide on writing a press release.
		- Just remember that the focus of this is to get a link. If your press release is announcing something newsworthy and it gets picked up by other media then fantastic! But don’t bank on it.
		- Once written, submit your press release via your choice of press release announcement services. You can get them submitted free, or pay more to get greater exposure. Your options will be dictated by your budget. I’ve found that using the free ones most of the time are OK. When you’ve got something really newsworthy, think about using the paid version.
		- Here are some sites that provide press release announcement services:
		- PRWeb.comPR.com PRBuzz.com PRLog.org

		- Presentation prep for talks at schools/meetings/organisations

		- As your traffic grows you may need to upgrade to a dedicated server or a virtual private server (VPS).  This web site is hosted by [ServInt](http://www.servint.net/index.php?refid=EDE327487180).  I’ve hosted this site with them since day one, and they’ve been a truly awesome host.  What I like most about them is that they have a smooth upgrade path as my traffic keeps growing.  I’ve gone through several upgrades with them already, and all have been seamless.  The nice thing about having your own server is that you can put as many sites on it as the server can handle.  I have several sites running on my server, and it doesn’t cost me any additional hosting fees to add another site

		  collapsed:: true
			- List your site on Portals – There are a growing number of sites which exclusively list blogs. If you want people to find you its worth submitting your blog to be listed on them. Some focus on specific topics while others list blogs on a wide variety of topics (like [Eaton Web](http://portal.eatonweb.com/) and [Globe of Blogs](http://www.globeofblogs.com/)). Other portals like [BlogShares](http://www.blogshares.com/) and [Blog Street](http://www.blogstreet.com/bin/add.cgi) also list a lot of blogs in different ways which might increase your blogs profile.
			- Blog Search Engines and Indexes – Get yourself registered on sites like [Blogdex](http://blogdex.net/), [Technorati](http://www.technorati.com/),[Popdex](http://popdex.com/) and [Daypop](http://www.daypop.com/blogrank/) (they require RSS I think). These sites have features that allow people to search for blog entries via topic and keywords. They also list the most popular recent topics and each have other interesting features which can enhance your blogging experience.
			- Add an RSS feed to your blog – more and more people are reading blogs without ever visiting them through News Aggregators that pick up information using RSS. Whilst this does not guarantee those reading through aggregators will visit your blog (and therefore see your Adsense Ads) it certainly increases the chances

		- Hi ,One thing I dislike about blogging is that we often spend a lot of time writing great material, only for it a few days later to be "lost forever" in the archives. There are a few things you can do, however, to make sure your awesome archived content gets read again and again:Make sure you have an archive page setup to easily access older posts.You can link back to old blog posts in your new ones, if they relate to the subject.Enable a "Related Posts" plugin that will automatically show related posts at the bottom of each of your posts. (I use the "Yet Another Related Posts Plugin", which can be found in the Wordpress plugin directory)You can create links to some of your top archives posts in your sidebar.You can include links to some of your best, lesser known posts in some of your newsletter follow-up messages, just like this one.
		- buy easy to remember domains for each call-to-action e.g. patsfacebook, ebook, newsletter etc
		- When car buyers had to customize cars, the more choices they had to make in the beginning, the more likely they were to stick with default options at the end. By manipulating the order of options (so that complex choices like picking one of 25 colors preceded simpler choices like picking one of two engines), dealers could bump the average price of a car up by up to 1500 Euros.
		- booksummaries
		- Split test optin box saying newsletter or socialtriggers free updates (seems less like a marketing autoresponder)
		- When traffic, no of comments etc plateaus then don't keep just publishing blog posts - expand with podcast, YouTube etc.
		- Post - what was your first impression of my website?
		- The other thing to consider is closing comments completely. I’ve done this on a few big content blogs and instead focused on a nice opt-in form and the interactions happening on Facebook. For a while Mehdi from Strong Lifts did this with his blog articles and instead pushed the discussion to his forum. Now the forum has grown so much he is charging for membership and making a great profit!
		- (Update Sep ’11: I’ve since shut off my contact form and now encourage all readers to connect with me via my [Facebook page](http://facebook.com/celestinechua) instead.)
		- If you don’t want to do that then I would try two things: asking questions in your blog post titles and then making sure you don’t totally exhaust the topic. I always try to leave a few ends open for discussion even when I write really long and in-depth posts.
		- TO SHOOT SIDE-BY-SIDE VIDEOS ONLINE EASILY
		- Ecamm Call Recorder: This is a super simple way to record Skype Video Calls on a Mac. This is the amazingly simple software I use to record interviews on Skype. It is literally one button and you are off to the races, plus some cool bonuses that come with it allowing you to change your file into multiple formats such as MP3. Best part is it is designed to be ridiculously simple so you don’t need certification from NASA to figure it out. Check it out right here.
		- VodBurner: This is the best option for PCs. I don’t have a PC personally, but I’ve asked around to everyone I know who uses a PC and they love VodBurner. What’s also cool about it is you can edit within the platform if you want to. Cool. Check it out right here.
		- GoToMeeting: In terms of video quality, I’ve noticed nothing better than GoToMeeting to shoot interviews because it now comes with HD Faces. You can record on a Mac by using GoToMeeting and ScreenFlow and I believe on PC you can record right from GoToMeeting or use Camtasia. Best part is your interviewee doesn’t need an account. They just click the link, accept a couple of things and boom! Check it out right here and click the “Try It Free” button and enter the promo code RISE for 45 days free. Whoop!
		- BLOGGING, VIDEO & WEB TOOLS
		- LeadPlayer: An amazing plugin that basically pimps YouTube when you embed videos on your site. You can create opt-in boxes, change opt-in boxes in ALL your videos, and keep traffic on your site as opposed to kicking it back to YouTube. Seriously one of the most amazing tools I’ve seen in a long time. Check out more about it right here.
		- ScreenFlow: For Mac, there is no simpler way to turn your screen into videos. I use Screenflow for so many things it is ridiculous, including shooting ads and sponsor plugs for the show, shooting the intro and conclusion and even editing The Rise To The Top (so much easier than iMovie, even though iMovie is really easy, which is saying a lot). Check it out right here.
		- Wistia: Best place to host your videos and a very sexy player you can use on your site. I use Wistia to host The Rise To The Top videos. It’s just so darn simple. You don’t need to be a tech genius to get your videos uploaded and shared and you don’t have to be a math professor to figure out if your videos are being watched (and are effective). They have great analytics, simple sharing and a really neat player that you can customize without a tech degree (yes, I said neat). Check it out here.
		- GoToMyPC: All about the virtual lifestyle. Never be tethered to just your computer. I use it to access everything on my computer from my iPad and iPhone as well as any other random computer I might be in front of. SWEET. Check it out right here and click the “Try It Free” button and enter the promo code RISE for 45 days free. Whoop!

If you are creating membership sites, digital products, etc. you can’t beat it and I’m using it myself for Create Awesome Interviews. More often than not, guests of the show use WishList (examples: Brian Moran, Sean Malarkey etc.). Check it out right here.

For example, in a recent interview on the show Lewis Howes revealed that the most successful way they promoted their latest product launch was webinars, which converted into over $500,000 in sales in one month. One tool he uses and so do other top dogs is Stealth Seminar, which allows you to create one webinar and play it over, and over, and over again to keep those sales rolling in.Check it out right here.

Every single one recommended OptimizePress for its ease of use, easy templates and numerous other badass features. Simple wins. Check it out right here.

		- Astrill VPN - If you’re in certain countries (like China) good luck getting on any social media sites without a VPN.  All of my friends over there swear by Astrill.  They also have a free one week trial – so check it out.  Even if you aren’t in China, I recommend using this for the sake of security.
		- [Prey](https://preyproject.com/) - Track your stolen laptop or phone as soon as it’s connected to the internet. Remote wipe all of the data.  If your stuff gets stolen, this is your best chance of finding it.
		- Skype Call Recorder - Super simple app for Skype that allows you to record your conversations.  Great if you’re doing interviews.
		- Caffeine - Mac only app that simply keeps your computer from going to sleep. Easy to toggle on and off, and is perfect when you’re doing things like webinars or other interviews.
		- Better Touch Tool - I’m not a fan of some of the gestures that came along with OSX Mountain Lion.  This solves that problem and let’s you pretty much create whatever touchpad gestures you want.
		- Mars Edit - Edit and format WordPress blog posts offline with this super useful desktop app.  I find myself using this on trains or buses all the time if I don’t have wifi.
		- Visual Website Optimizer - Not the cheapest service at around $50/month, but its the easiest way I’ve found to edit pages and split test offers.
		- [Pretty Links ](http://prettylinkpro.com/)- Make all of your ugly affiliate links look nice, and well, pretty! I use the free “lite” version and it works just fine.
		- Trello – Free solution for team collaboration. I actually like this better than paid solutions like Basecamp.
		- [Box](https://www.box.com/) – Need to transfer really large files between people you’re working with? Box works great.

		- Web Content Studio (WCS)

		- Other Productivity / Misc. Tools
		- Call Recorder for Skype: I use this to record all the Skype interviews I do here and elsewhere. It’s easy to set up, solid and inexpensive.
		- ScreenFlow: I use this for recording my screen while delivering webinars and presentations. It’s how I developed Traffic School, The Hustle Project and other classes and presentations we’ve done. ScreenFlow is one of the most important tools I use on a regular basis.
		- Align RSS Images (Download Link)
		- If you align images with text in your blog posts then they won’t actually stay aligned (left or right) in your blogs RSS feed for people who are reading your content using the likes of Google Reader. Instead, any graphics you use will sit above your text on their own line, which usually doesn’t look too good. The Align RSS Images plugin fixes this problem for you.
		- cbnet Ping Optimizer (Download Link)
		- If you include a lot of resources in your WordPress pingback section (this basically notifies certain services that you’ve written a new article) then you need to be careful. Any time you edit the post it will re-ping those services with your changes. Too many pings and there’s a chance you could get banned from the respective portals. This plugin minimises the number of pings your blog sends, to counteract this potential issue.
		- Comment Redirect (Download Link)
		- Created by one of my favourite plugin developers, Joost De Valk (Yoast), comment redirect allows you to send visitors to any page on your site after leaving their first ever comment. I personally send them to a page where I thank them for commenting, and then offer ways to subscribe to my blog so they can get future updates. Leave a comment on ViperChill.com for the first time and you can see this in action.
		- FD Feedburner Plugin (Download Link)
		- On the next page I’ll be looking at some services to use on your blog with one of them being Feedburner. This allows you to track how (and how many) people are subscribing to your site. The FD Feedburner plugin allows you to easily redirect your default WordPress blog feed URL to your new Feedburner URL (if you are using their service) to ensure that your readers aren’t missing any updates and your feed count is accurate.
		- Outbound Links (Download Link)
		- This plugin simply makes outbound links to other websites open in a new browser tab. I find this especially useful when I reference other websites in a post, but need people to continue reading in order to understand why I linked that site. The plugin is very straightforward and does its intended job as expected.
		- ViperRep: Reputation Management for WordPress (Download Link)
		- Find out who’s talking about you, your brand or products online with Reputation Management for WordPress. This is a plugin I created myself after having the desire to see when other bloggers are talking about me and my website. It allows you to use as many phrases and to pull as many results as you wish and displays them clearly in your WordPress dashboard area.
		- Subscribe to Comments (Download Link)
		- This plugin simply adds a checkbox to your current blog comments and allows people to subscribe to get email updates when new comments are posted. This helps you to get more comments on your site, more returning visitors and also helps discussions form more freely on popular articles.
		- ViperBar (Download Link)
		- ViperBar was created by yours truly after wanting more features from other similar options out there. In short, ViperBar adds an attractive, eye-catching banner to the top of your website which gives people the option to get notified whenever you write a new blog post, thus helping to increase the number of subscribers you have. It works with Mail Chimp, Aweber, and Feedburner which are covered on the following page.
		- ViperFeed (Download Link)
		- Another plugin created by me, ViperFeed allows you to style your RSS feed footer in any way that you wish. This allows you to easily show other posts you’ve written or even giveaway freebies to subscribers. It also allows you to add an attractive divider bar which separates your post from the “footer” content.
		- ViperProof (Download Link)
		- My final plugin, ViperProof, was mentioned in the last update as an easy way to show off ‘social proof’ on your blog in order to encourage more people to subscribe and take notice of your website. You can see this in action in the bottom right corner of ViperChill.com where you see all of my blog stats.
		- Widget Context (Download Link)
		- In the last update I mentioned that you should look for a theme which is widget enabled so, if you’re a beginner to editing WordPress themes, you can customise your design with a little more ease. Widget context takes the benefits of Widgets to another level by allowing you to choose whether certain widgets show (or don’t show) on certain pages.
		- For example, on your article pages you may want to have a small bio about yourself and why you’re blogging in your sidebar. If you don’t want this to show in your sidebar when people are on your homepage then you can easily specify that with this addon. Simply go to Appearance >Widgets and click the drop-down arrow on any widget to tweak your settings.
		- WPTouch (Download Link)
		- WPTouch allows you to improve the appearance and usability of your WordPress blog for people who connect to your website on a mobile phone. Specifically, iOS (iPhone) and Android devices. The premium version also adds a number of customization options which let you make your mobile site look relevant and similar to your desktop-optimised design.
		- You’ll probably be pleased to hear that I’m now finished with my list of plugins. On the next page we’ll look at my recommended blogging services and a few final tweaks you should make to your blog…
		- Tweet Old Posts (plugin) randomly picks your older post based on the interval specified by you. The primary function of this plugin is to promote older blog posts by tweeting about them and getting more traffic.
		- [http://www.leangains.com](http://www.leangains.com/) - widgets on side, use his ones
		- -twitter and facebook (call to action - follow me on twitter for latest updates on blog posts and inspiring quotes daily!)

	- Plugins
	  collapsed:: true
		- Mindvalley Comments Moderator
		- How do you let someone moderate comments for you without giving them the ability to change posts or accidentally cause other trouble? Use this plug-in, that’s how. It creates a custom role that enables only Comment Moderation actions and pages.
		- Page Links ToAllows you to point WordPress pages or posts to a URL of your choosing. Good for setting up navigational links to non-WP sections of your site or to off-site resources. Since my content is scattered all over the place, this is very helpful.
		- Post-Plugin LibraryDoes nothing by itself but supplies common code for the Similar Posts, Recent Posts, Random Posts, and Recent Comments plugins. Make sure you have the latest version of this plugin.
		- Similar PostsDisplays a highly configurable list of related posts. Similarity can be based on any combination of word usage in the content, title, or tags. Don’t be disturbed if it takes a few moments to complete the installation — the plugin is indexing your posts. Instructions and help online. Requires the latest version of the Post-Plugin Library to be installed.
		- Subscribe To CommentsAllows readers to receive notifications of new comments that are posted to an entry. Based on version 1 from Scriptygoddess.
		- Twitter ToolsA complete integration between your WordPress blog and Twitter. Bring your tweets into your blog and pass your blog posts to Twitter. Show your tweets in your sidebar, and post tweets from your WordPress admin.
		- VaultPressProtect your content, themes, plugins, and settings with real-time backup and automated security scanning from VaultPress. Activate, enter your registration key, and never worry again. (Note: I pay for this one.)
		- WordPress Database BackupOn-demand backup of your WordPress database.
		- WP-FootnotesGenerate footnotes for posts.
		- WP Hide PostEnables a user to control the visibility of items on the blog by making posts and pages selectively hidden in different views throughout the blog, such as on the front page, category pages, search results, etc… The hidden item remains otherwise accessible directly using permalinks, and also visible to search engines as part of the sitemap (at least). This plugin enables new SEO possibilities for authors since it enables them to create new posts and pages without being forced to display them on their front and in feeds.
		- WP Super CacheVery fast caching plugin for WordPress. This is the only thing that could save the blog from crashing when I hit the front pages of Digg, StumbleUpon, etc.
		- And… that’s it!
